enrichment.short_description: Around was a floating-bubble video calling app that cut meeting fatigue for remote teams; Miro acquired it in 2022 and sunset it in 2025.
enrichment.company_size: ~35 employees (at the time of Miro's 2022 acquisition)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Around was a lightweight video calling app that reimagined what a call could look like: instead of a full-screen grid of rectangular tiles, participants appeared as small floating circles that could be dragged anywhere on screen and left running while people worked in other apps. Co-founded in 2018 by Dominik Zane and Pavel Serbajlo, with Matt Zakutny, the company spent nearly two years in stealth before launching publicly in March 2020, just as the pandemic pushed remote and hybrid work into the mainstream. Around went on to raise more than 15 million dollars in funding from investors including Floodgate, Initialized Capital, Credo Ventures, and the Slack Fund, and grew to a team of roughly three dozen people.

The company's central bet was that most video-calling fatigue came from the format, not the underlying technology: staring at a grid of faces all day is exhausting, and closing a call to focus on other work can feel isolating for a distributed team. Around's floating bubbles, AI-driven face framing, and ultrasonic EchoPrevention technology were built to let people keep a call open in the background, more like sitting near a coworker than attending a formal meeting. In October 2022, Around was acquired by the visual-collaboration platform Miro, with deal terms not publicly disclosed, and rebranded as Around by Miro Labs. Miro kept the product free for all users while folding its best ideas into its own video tools, and ultimately discontinued the standalone Around app on March 31, 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Around Is For
paragraphs: Around was built for distributed and hybrid teams who wanted a call to feel like being in the next room rather than attending a scheduled meeting. Pair-programming duos, co-working friends, and small startup teams used it to keep a lightweight video connection open for hours at a time, popping in and out of conversation the way colleagues would in a shared physical office rather than dialing in and out of formal blocks on a calendar.

It also suited creative and product teams who valued the playful, informal touches layered on top of a genuinely useful AI camera and audio system, things like reactions, camera filters, icebreaker sounds, and small multiplayer games that made spontaneous check-ins feel less like work. After the Miro acquisition, it was a natural fit for teams already using Miro's whiteboard for brainstorming and workshops, since it let them add a synchronous video layer without ever leaving the collaborative canvas they were already working in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: The most important consideration today is that Around no longer exists as a product anyone can buy or adopt: it was sunset on March 31, 2025, and its accounts, recordings, and third-party integrations were shut down along with it. Anyone evaluating video-calling tools now should look at Miro's built-in Video Calls, which absorbed features like EchoPrevention, reactions, filters, and floating mode, or at mainstream alternatives such as Zoom, Google Meet, or Microsoft Teams.

Even when it was active, Around was a poor fit for large or formal meetings. It intentionally capped participant counts, offered no dial-in or PSTN support, had limited recording and admin controls compared with mainstream platforms, and lacked the compliance and enterprise-management features that larger organizations expect. Companies running large all-hands calls, webinars, sales demos, or anything requiring phone-based access generally chose a heavier-duty platform instead, even during Around's most popular years.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Around's signature feature was its floating circular video windows: instead of a rectangular call window competing for screen space, each participant appeared as a small round bubble that could be dragged anywhere on the desktop and stayed visible while using other apps. An AI auto-framing system kept each person's face centered and in focus inside that circle, effectively acting as an automatic camera operator so nobody had to think about their webcam position.

On the audio side, Around's EchoPrevention used ultrasonic tones to detect nearby open microphones and automatically suppress feedback loops, a common pain point when several hybrid teammates sat in the same conference room without headphones. It paired this with noise cancellation that filtered out barking dogs, sirens, and other background sound, and it shipped with lightweight social extras: emoji reactions, camera filters, icebreaker sounds, ambient background music dubbed vibes, and simple multiplayer games meant for informal hangouts rather than agenda-driven meetings.

Around also supported screen sharing, added a beta recording feature in 2022, and offered native apps across Mac, Windows, the web, iOS, and Android, with the mobile apps arriving in mid-2021. After Miro's acquisition, it gained the ability to launch and join calls directly from a Miro board, bringing video and visual whiteboard collaboration into a single window instead of two separate tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Around Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Around felt closer to leaving a door open than scheduling a meeting: teammates joined a persistent room, their face bubbles appeared floating over whatever else was on screen, and conversations could start and stop organically, much like a hallway chat in a physical office rather than a calendar-blocked call. That ambient, always-on quality, combined with a genuinely useful camera and audio experience, is what earned Around a loyal following among early remote-work adopters and a wave of enthusiastic press coverage in 2020 and 2021.

Under Miro's ownership, Around remained free for all users while its team worked on integrating the product with Miro's canvas. By 2024, Miro had built a very similar experience, floating mode, reactions, filters, EchoPrevention, and recording, directly into its own Video Calls product, and it retired the standalone Around app on March 31, 2025. For anyone who misses the format, Around's core ideas now live on inside Miro rather than as an independent product people can sign up for today.

features.description: Each participant appears as a small round video window that can be dragged anywhere on screen, instead of a full rectangular call window.

features.title: AI auto-framing
features.description: An AI camera system automatically keeps each person's face centered and in focus within their bubble, acting like an automatic camera operator.

features.title: EchoPrevention
features.description: Ultrasonic audio detects nearby open microphones in the same room and suppresses feedback, so hybrid teammates can sit together without headphones.

features.title: Noise cancellation
features.description: AI-based audio filtering removes background sounds like barking dogs, sirens, and traffic during calls.

features.title: Reactions, filters, and icebreakers
features.description: Emoji reactions, camera filters, and icebreaker sounds add lightweight, informal touches to everyday calls.

features.title: Ambient vibes and mini-games
features.description: Optional background music and simple multiplayer games designed for casual hangouts rather than agenda-driven meetings.

features.title: Screen sharing
features.description: Standard screen sharing that kept participants' face bubbles visible on top of shared content.

features.title: Recording
features.description: A recording feature (introduced in beta in 2022) let teams capture calls for later reference.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native apps for Mac, Windows, and the web, plus iOS and Android mobile apps launched in mid-2021.

features.title: Miro board integration
features.description: After the 2022 acquisition, calls could be launched and joined directly from a Miro whiteboard.

pros: Distinctive floating-bubble format reduced the fatigue of staring at a grid of video tiles all day
AI face-framing kept presenters visible and well-composed without manual webcam adjustments
EchoPrevention solved the common problem of audio feedback when several people share a room
Lightweight, always-on feel encouraged spontaneous, hallway-style conversations for remote teams
Simple, playful interface with reactions, filters, and icebreakers that lowered the barrier to informal check-ins
cons: Discontinued on March 31, 2025 — no longer available as a standalone product to sign up for
Capped participant counts made it unsuitable for large all-hands meetings or webinars
No phone dial-in or PSTN support for participants without a computer or app
Recording and admin/compliance controls were limited compared with mainstream platforms like Zoom or Teams
Best features (like EchoPrevention) depended on decent hardware and microphone quality to work well

faqs.question: Is Around still available today?
faqs.answer: No. Around was sunset on March 31, 2025. Miro, which acquired the company in October 2022, retired the standalone app and folded features like floating mode, reactions, and EchoPrevention into its own Video Calls product.

faqs.question: Who acquired Around, and when?
faqs.answer: Miro, the visual-collaboration platform, acquired Around in October 2022. The deal terms were not publicly disclosed, and the product was rebranded as Around by Miro Labs before eventually being discontinued.

faqs.question: What made Around's interface different from Zoom or Google Meet?
faqs.answer: Instead of a rectangular grid of video tiles, Around showed each participant as a small floating circle that could be moved around the screen and left open while multitasking in other apps.

faqs.question: Can I still get Around's features somewhere else?
faqs.answer: Many of Around's signature features, including floating mode, reactions, filters, EchoPrevention, and recording, were rebuilt directly into Miro's own Video Calls product before Around was sunset.

faqs.question: What platforms did Around support?
faqs.answer: Around offered native apps for Mac, Windows, and the web, plus iOS and Android mobile apps that launched in mid-2021.

use_cases.title: Remote pair programming and co-working
use_cases.description: Engineers kept a persistent Around bubble open for hours to collaborate on code and ask quick questions without scheduling a formal call.

use_cases.title: Ambient virtual office presence

use_cases.title: Creative collaboration alongside a whiteboard

use_cases.title: Quick standups and 1:1 check-ins
use_cases.description: Small startup teams used Around's low-friction bubbles for brief daily standups and one-on-ones without a full-screen meeting interrupting their workflow.

enrichment.short_description: Ashby is an all-in-one ATS combining applicant tracking, sourcing, scheduling, and analytics for high-growth tech companies.
enrichment.starting_price: From $400/month (Foundations plan, up to 100 employees); custom quote above that

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ashby is a modern applicant tracking system (ATS) and recruiting platform built for high-growth technology companies. Founded in 2018 by Benjamin Encz and Abhik Pramanik and based in San Francisco, Ashby set out to replace the fragmented stack of legacy ATS tools, spreadsheets, and point solutions that many recruiting teams cobble together, with a single unified product spanning sourcing, applicant tracking, interview scheduling, and reporting.

Unlike many older-generation ATS platforms, Ashby was built with analytics as a first-class feature rather than an afterthought, giving talent teams pipeline, funnel-conversion, and recruiter-productivity dashboards natively instead of relying on third-party BI exports. The platform also bundles a sourcing CRM and outbound email/sequencing tools, letting recruiters build and nurture candidate pipelines without leaving the product.

Ashby has grown into one of the more widely adopted ATS options among venture-backed startups and scale-ups, and has layered in AI-assisted features over time, including automatic interview note-taking and summaries and AI-assisted candidate sourcing, while keeping its configurable interview and hiring-process engine as the backbone of the product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Ashby Is For
paragraphs: Ashby is aimed squarely at in-house talent acquisition teams at venture-backed startups and scale-ups, roughly Series A through pre-IPO, that are hiring at volume and need a single system of record for the entire funnel. Companies that have outgrown Greenhouse, Lever, or a spreadsheet-based process, and want a more modern interface plus built-in reporting, tend to be a strong fit.

It particularly suits recruiting leaders who care about hiring analytics — time-to-hire, source performance, interviewer load, funnel conversion — and don't want to stitch that data together manually. Technical and engineering-heavy hiring organizations also benefit from Ashby's structured interview kits, scorecards, and assessment integrations.

Because pricing is based on total company headcount and contracts are typically annual, Ashby tends to work best for organizations with a dedicated recruiting or People Ops function that can own the system, rather than a single hiring manager making occasional ad hoc hires.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small companies, solo founders, or teams making only a handful of hires a year will likely find Ashby's pricing and annual-contract model heavier than necessary. There is no free plan or free trial, so lightweight or occasional hiring needs are often better served by simpler, cheaper ATS tools.

Large, non-tech enterprises with complex multi-country HR/compliance requirements or deep legacy HRIS and payroll integration needs may find that more established enterprise ATS/HCM suites offer more mature compliance tooling out of the box.

Teams that want to try before they buy should also weigh the lack of a self-serve trial: evaluating Ashby means going through a sales demo and proof-of-concept rather than signing up independently, which can be friction for smaller or more DIY-oriented teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Ashby offers a configurable applicant tracking system with customizable pipelines, interview stages, and hiring-process automation, alongside a sourcing CRM for building talent pools, running email outreach sequences, and tracking passive-candidate engagement over time.

Scheduling automation handles interview-panel coordination across calendars and time zones, while the analytics layer provides dashboards on pipeline health, funnel conversion, source effectiveness, and interviewer activity that can be sliced by requisition, department, or recruiter. Ashby also includes a careers-page builder, offer-approval workflows, and an open API and webhooks that connect to job boards, assessment providers, calendars, and HRIS platforms.

More recently, Ashby has added AI-driven features, including an AI meeting notetaker that records and summarizes interviews and AI-assisted tools for surfacing and filtering candidates, layered on top of the same structured-interview and scorecard framework that anchors the hiring process.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Ashby Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, recruiters and hiring managers generally describe Ashby's interface as fast, clean, and noticeably more modern than legacy ATS tools, with less clicking required to move candidates through a pipeline or pull a report. The reporting experience is frequently cited as a differentiator versus competitors that require exporting data to third-party BI tools.

Onboarding typically involves a guided setup with Ashby's team to configure interview processes, permissions, and integrations, and the vendor is known for being responsive to customer feedback and shipping frequent product updates. Support and account management are generally rated well by customers, particularly during initial rollout.

Tradeoffs some teams report include a learning curve around the platform's flexibility, since there are many ways to configure a hiring process, add-on pricing for features like advanced scheduling or the AI notetaker, and the commitment of an annual contract without a self-serve trial to test fit beforehand.

Strong native analytics and reporting without needing external BI tools
Combines ATS, sourcing CRM, scheduling, and analytics in one product
Deep configurability for interview processes and hiring workflows
Responsive vendor support and frequent product updates
cons: No free trial or free plan; evaluation requires a sales demo and proof-of-concept
Pricing is based on total company headcount, which can rise as a company grows headcount even without more hiring
Some advanced features, like the AI notetaker and advanced scheduling, are paid add-ons
Annual contract commitment rather than month-to-month flexibility
Configuration flexibility can create a learning curve for new admins

faqs.question: Does Ashby offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: No. Ashby does not offer a self-serve free trial or free plan; prospective customers evaluate the platform through a sales demo and proof-of-concept process.

faqs.question: How much does Ashby cost?
faqs.answer: Ashby's Foundations plan starts at roughly $400 per month for companies with up to 100 employees, with a discount for annual billing. Pricing for larger companies (101-1,000+ employees) is custom-quoted based on total headcount.

faqs.question: What is Ashby used for?
faqs.answer: Ashby is used as an all-in-one recruiting platform, combining applicant tracking, candidate sourcing and CRM, interview scheduling automation, and hiring analytics in a single system.

faqs.question: Who founded Ashby and when?
faqs.answer: Ashby was founded in 2018 by Benjamin Encz and Abhik Pramanik and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Ashby integrate with other HR and recruiting tools?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ashby offers integrations with job boards, assessment providers, calendars, and HRIS platforms, plus an open API and webhooks for custom connections.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based EHR, practice management, and revenue cycle management platform that streamlines clinical, billing, and patient engagement workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: athenaOne is athenahealth's flagship cloud-based platform that combines electronic health records (EHR), practice management, revenue cycle management (RCM), and patient engagement tools into a single connected system for medical practices and health systems. athenahealth was founded in 1997 and is headquartered in Watertown, Massachusetts. The company built its reputation on medical billing and claims expertise before expanding into full EHR and practice management software, and that revenue-cycle DNA still shapes how athenaOne is positioned today.

Rather than being sold as static, self-hosted software, athenaOne is delivered as a continuously updated cloud service. athenahealth maintains a shared rules engine that tracks payer requirements, coding changes, and regulatory updates (such as MIPS and value-based care measures) across its client base, then pushes those updates to every practice on the platform. The product suite includes named modules such as athenaClinicals (EHR), athenaCollector (practice management and billing), and athenaCommunicator (patient communication), all unified under the athenaOne umbrella.

The platform targets ambulatory practices of nearly any size, from independent physician offices to large multi-specialty groups and hospital-affiliated networks, and is available across many medical specialties. Its core pitch is reducing the administrative burden of running a practice — documentation, scheduling, claims submission, and collections — so clinicians can focus more on patient care.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who athenaOne Is For
paragraphs: athenaOne is best suited for outpatient medical practices — from solo and small-group physician offices to larger multi-specialty groups and health-system-affiliated clinics — that want an all-in-one EHR, scheduling, and billing system without managing on-premise servers or a large internal IT team. Practices that want to outsource significant parts of their revenue cycle (claims scrubbing, denial follow-up, and collections work) rather than fully staffing that function in-house tend to get the most value from athenaOne's bundled RCM services.

It also appeals to practices that prioritize staying current with payer rule changes and quality reporting requirements (such as MIPS) without manually tracking every update themselves, since athenahealth centrally maintains its payer rules engine across its entire client network. Organizations expanding to multiple locations or specialties often choose athenaOne for its ability to scale and its broad, established base of payer and clearinghouse connections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Practices with very tight, predictable software budgets may want to look elsewhere, since athenaOne pricing is quote-based and, for RCM-inclusive plans, is often structured around a percentage of collections rather than a flat subscription fee — making total cost harder to forecast than with a simple per-provider license. High-volume practices with strong in-house billing teams may find a collections-based fee less cost-effective than a flat-rate EHR alternative.

Practices that need deep, highly customized workflow configuration or on-premise/self-hosted deployment options should also consider alternatives, since athenaOne is a shared, standardized cloud platform designed for broad applicability rather than heavy per-client customization. Inpatient hospital systems needing a full acute-care EHR (rather than ambulatory-focused tools) typically look toward enterprise inpatient EHR vendors instead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: athenaOne's core feature set spans clinical, administrative, and financial workflows. On the clinical side, athenaClinicals provides charting, e-prescribing, order management, and clinical decision support, along with a mobile app that lets clinicians document visits, review charts, and manage tasks from a phone or tablet — including AI-powered ambient documentation (Ambient Notes) that generates draft visit notes from patient-clinician conversations.

On the administrative and financial side, athenaCollector handles scheduling, eligibility verification, claims submission, and denial management, backed by athenahealth's payer rules engine, which is updated centrally and applied across its client base to help reduce claim rejections. athenaCommunicator adds patient-facing engagement tools such as appointment reminders, online scheduling, and secure messaging, complemented by the separate athenaPatient mobile app and patient portal.

Additional capabilities include population health and quality-reporting tools for value-based care programs, interoperability features for exchanging records with other health systems, and analytics dashboards that give practice leaders visibility into financial and operational performance.

long_form_blocks.heading: The athenaOne Experience
paragraphs: Because athenaOne is a cloud-native, continuously updated platform, practices generally do not manage servers, manual software updates, or version upgrades themselves — athenahealth handles maintenance and rolls out payer and regulatory rule changes centrally. This reduces IT overhead but also means practices have less control over the exact timing or configuration of changes compared with self-hosted systems.

Onboarding typically involves a structured implementation process, including data migration, staff training, and configuration to a practice's specialty and workflows, which can take weeks depending on practice size and complexity. Ongoing support is delivered largely through athenahealth's own success and billing-services teams, reflecting the platform's roots as a revenue-cycle-first company.

Reviews of the day-to-day experience are mixed: many practices credit athenaOne with reducing claim denials and administrative workload thanks to its billing expertise and rules engine, while others cite a learning curve when transitioning from legacy systems and note that customer support responsiveness can vary. Overall, the experience tends to be strongest for practices that lean into the bundled RCM services rather than using athenaOne purely as a standalone EHR.

faqs.question: What is athenaOne?
faqs.answer: athenaOne is athenahealth's cloud-based platform that combines an electronic health record (EHR), practice management, revenue cycle management, and patient engagement tools into a single system for medical practices.

faqs.question: How much does athenaOne cost?
faqs.answer: athenahealth does not publish standard pricing; costs are quote-based and vary by practice size, specialty, and modules selected. Plans that include revenue cycle services are often structured around a percentage of a practice's collections rather than a flat subscription fee.

faqs.question: Is athenaOne cloud-based?
faqs.answer: Yes. athenaOne is delivered entirely as a cloud (SaaS) platform, so practices don't need to host servers themselves, and athenahealth manages ongoing updates, including payer rule and regulatory changes.

faqs.question: Does athenaOne have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes. athenahealth offers the athenaOne mobile app for clinicians on iOS and Android, including an AI-powered ambient documentation feature, plus a separate athenaPatient app for patients.

faqs.question: What practice sizes and specialties does athenaOne support?
faqs.answer: athenaOne is used by practices ranging from solo physician offices to large multi-specialty groups and hospital-affiliated networks, and it supports a wide range of medical specialties.

enrichment.short_description: Auth0 is a cloud identity platform that adds login, SSO, and MFA to apps via APIs and SDKs, now part of Okta.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Auth0 is an identity and access management (IAM) platform that lets development teams add authentication and authorization to web, mobile, and API-based applications without building that infrastructure from scratch. It handles the plumbing of modern login — username/password, social sign-in, single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and passwordless flows — behind a hosted 'Universal Login' page and a set of APIs, SDKs, and quickstarts for most popular languages and frameworks.

The company was founded in 2013 by Eugenio Pace and Matias Woloski in Bellevue, Washington, growing into one of the best-known standalone identity-as-a-service vendors before being acquired by Okta in 2021. It now operates as part of Okta's Customer Identity Cloud, marketed as 'Auth0 by Okta,' while continuing to run as a distinct product with its own dashboard, documentation, and developer-first positioning.

At its core, Auth0 is built on open identity standards — OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and SAML — so it can broker logins against social providers (Google, Facebook, Apple, GitHub, etc.), enterprise directories (Active Directory, Azure AD, LDAP, other SAML/OIDC providers), and its own user database, all through a single integration point.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Auth0 Is For
paragraphs: Auth0 is aimed squarely at software engineering teams — from solo developers to enterprise platform teams — who need production-grade authentication but don't want to own the security burden of building and maintaining it in-house. It's a natural fit for SaaS companies adding customer login (B2C), and for B2B products that need to federate logins with each customer's own corporate identity provider.

It also suits organizations that need to check compliance and security boxes quickly: MFA, breached-password detection, brute-force protection, and audit logs are available out of the box rather than requiring custom engineering. Enterprises with complex requirements — multiple brands, multiple regions, strict role-based access control, or a need to support many different enterprise SSO connections — are a common use case, since Auth0's per-connection federation model scales well to that complexity.

Startups that want to get an MVP into users' hands quickly benefit from the free tier and prebuilt UI, while larger engineering organizations often adopt it specifically so their own developers can stop maintaining bespoke auth code and instead consume it as a managed API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small or budget-constrained projects with simple needs — a single app with basic email/password login and no compliance requirements — may find Auth0 more platform than they need; simpler or bundled options (such as the auth features built into Firebase, Supabase, or a framework's own auth library) can be faster to stand up and cheaper at low scale.

Teams that require full self-hosting or complete control over the identity data plane for regulatory, air-gapped, or data-sovereignty reasons will find Auth0's primarily multi-tenant, cloud-hosted model restrictive; open-source, self-managed alternatives like Keycloak are usually a better match there (Auth0 does offer private/dedicated deployment options, but these sit at the high end of its enterprise pricing).

Organizations wary of vendor lock-in to a proprietary identity layer, or wanting to avoid being tied into Okta's broader ecosystem post-acquisition, may also prefer to evaluate that tradeoff carefully before committing, since migrating user identities and custom rules off Auth0 later can involve real engineering effort.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Auth0's core is Universal Login, a customizable, hosted authentication page that supports social logins, enterprise connections (SAML, OIDC, WS-Fed, AD/LDAP), and traditional username/password, so a single integration can support many login methods. Around that sits multi-factor authentication (push, OTP, SMS, WebAuthn/passkeys), passwordless login via email or SMS, and account-linking across identity providers.

For developers, Auth0 exposes extensibility through Actions (and legacy Rules), which let teams run custom logic during login, token issuance, or user registration — for example, enriching tokens with claims, calling external APIs, or enforcing custom business rules. Role-based access control and fine-grained authorization support B2B and multi-tenant apps, while Attack Protection features (breached-password detection, brute-force and bot detection, suspicious IP throttling) add security without custom code.

Beyond authentication, the platform provides machine-to-machine and API authorization (client credentials/OAuth2 flows) for securing backend services, custom domains and branded login pages, detailed logs and log streaming to SIEM/analytics tools, and a broad library of SDKs and quickstarts covering SPAs, native mobile apps, regular web apps, and backend APIs across most mainstream languages and frameworks.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Auth0 Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, most teams experience Auth0 through its dashboard (for configuring applications, connections, and rules), its documentation and quickstarts (which are widely regarded as thorough and a major reason for its early popularity), and its APIs/SDKs, which developers wire into their own login screens or embed via the hosted Universal Login page.

The tradeoff many teams navigate is pricing that scales with monthly active users (MAUs) rather than a flat seat fee — inexpensive and even free at low volume, but a cost that needs to be modeled as a product grows, especially once enterprise connections, advanced MFA, or custom domains push a team into higher tiers. Since the 2021 Okta acquisition, Auth0 has increasingly been positioned alongside Okta's own workforce identity products, with some roadmap and pricing changes reflecting that broader integration, which is worth checking against current needs before committing long-term.

Overall, the experience is generally described as 'identity that just works' for developers who'd rather not become identity-security experts themselves, at the cost of depending on an external, usage-priced service for a piece of infrastructure that's foundational to the application.

features.title: Universal Login
features.description: A hosted, customizable login page that centralizes password, social, and enterprise sign-in behind one integration point.

features.title: Social & enterprise connections
features.description: Prebuilt federation with social providers (Google, Facebook, Apple, GitHub, etc.) and enterprise identity systems (SAML, OIDC, Active Directory, LDAP).

features.title: Multi-factor authentication
features.description: Push notifications, OTP apps, SMS, and WebAuthn/passkey-based MFA to add a second factor without custom development.

features.title: Passwordless login
features.description: Email- and SMS-based magic-link or one-time-code login flows that remove passwords from the equation entirely.

features.title: Actions and Rules
features.description: Custom JavaScript logic that runs during login, signup, or token issuance to enrich tokens, call external APIs, or enforce business rules.

features.title: Role-based & fine-grained authorization
features.description: Assign roles and permissions to users and expose them in tokens for use by downstream applications and APIs.

features.title: Attack protection
features.description: Built-in breached-password detection, brute-force protection, and bot/anomaly detection to harden login endpoints.

features.title: Machine-to-machine & API authorization
features.description: OAuth 2.0 client-credentials flows and scoped access tokens for securing backend services and APIs.

features.title: Custom domains and branding
features.description: Serve the login experience on the app's own domain with custom styling to match its brand.

features.title: SDKs, quickstarts, and logs
features.description: A broad library of SDKs for web, mobile, and backend frameworks, plus detailed logs and log-streaming for monitoring and compliance.

pros: Mature, standards-based platform (OAuth2/OIDC/SAML) with broad SDK and framework support
Free tier makes it easy to prototype and validate before committing budget
Strong out-of-the-box security features like MFA, breached-password detection, and anomaly protection
Extensive documentation and quickstarts that shorten integration time for developers
Handles complex enterprise SSO federation well for B2B products
cons: Pricing scales with monthly active users, which can become costly as an app grows
Primarily a multi-tenant cloud service, so teams needing full self-hosting/data sovereignty have limited options
Advanced features (custom domains, enterprise connections, advanced MFA) are often gated behind higher-priced tiers
Migrating away from Auth0 later can require nontrivial engineering effort around user data and custom rules
Post-Okta-acquisition roadmap and pricing changes require teams to periodically re-check fit against current needs

faqs.question: What is Auth0 used for?
faqs.answer: Auth0 is used by developers to add login, single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and API authorization to web, mobile, and backend applications without building that infrastructure themselves.

faqs.question: Is Auth0 free to use?
faqs.answer: Auth0 offers a free tier with a limited number of monthly active users, suitable for prototypes and small apps; paid plans are required as usage or feature needs (like enterprise SSO or advanced MFA) grow.

faqs.question: Is Auth0 still a separate company from Okta?
faqs.answer: Auth0 was acquired by Okta in 2021 and now operates as an Okta product line, often marketed as Auth0 by Okta, while retaining its own dashboard and developer-facing branding.

faqs.question: Does Auth0 support social login and enterprise SSO?
faqs.answer: Yes. It supports social identity providers such as Google, Facebook, and Apple, as well as enterprise federation via SAML, OIDC, Active Directory, and LDAP.

faqs.question: What are common alternatives to Auth0?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Okta's own workforce products, Firebase Authentication, Supabase Auth, AWS Cognito, and self-hosted open-source options like Keycloak.

use_cases.title: Customer login for a SaaS product
use_cases.description: Add sign-up, login, password reset, and social sign-in to a web or mobile SaaS app using Universal Login instead of building custom auth screens.

use_cases.title: B2B enterprise SSO
use_cases.description: Let business customers log in using their own corporate identity provider (Azure AD, Okta, other SAML/OIDC systems) so end users never need a separate password.
use_cases.audience: B2B SaaS companies serving enterprise customers

use_cases.title: Securing internal and partner APIs
use_cases.description: Use OAuth2 client-credentials flows and scoped access tokens to authenticate machine-to-machine calls between internal services or with partner integrations.

use_cases.title: Regulated or security-sensitive applications
use_cases.description: Apply MFA, breached-password detection, and detailed audit logging to meet security expectations in fintech, healthcare-adjacent, or other compliance-conscious products.
use_cases.audience: Teams in regulated or security-focused industries

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Auth0 review: pricing, features, pros and cons of Okta's developer identity platform for login, SSO, and MFA.

enrichment.short_description: Autodesk's unified construction management platform connecting design, preconstruction, and field teams through one common data environment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Autodesk Construction Cloud (ACC) is Autodesk's unified construction management platform, built to connect design, preconstruction, and field operations around a single common data environment. Rather than being built from scratch, ACC was assembled by folding together several products Autodesk acquired over the years — BIM 360, its original cloud collaboration platform; PlanGrid, a widely used field document and drawing management tool; Assemble Systems, a model-based quantification and cost management engine; and BuildingConnected, a bid management network — into one connected suite.

Today, ACC acts as the umbrella for Autodesk's construction-facing cloud products, sitting alongside BIM Collaborate (Autodesk's design coordination tool) within the broader Autodesk AEC (architecture, engineering, construction) ecosystem. It handles document control, RFIs, submittals, quality and safety inspections, punch lists, model coordination, cost management, and reporting, and it ties directly into Autodesk's design authoring tools such as Revit, AutoCAD, and Civil 3D.

Because of that lineage, ACC's core differentiator is depth of BIM integration: it's built to keep a project's 3D model, 2D drawings, and field data in sync, which positions it against competitors like Procore, Oracle Aconex, and Trimble/Viewpoint as the option most tightly coupled to Autodesk's design software stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Autodesk Construction Cloud Is For
paragraphs: ACC is aimed at general contractors, construction managers, and owners running capital construction programs who need a single system of record across design, preconstruction, and field execution. It's particularly well suited to teams already working inside the Autodesk ecosystem — firms using Revit, AutoCAD, or Civil 3D for design who want that model data to flow directly into field workflows without re-uploading or reformatting files.

Design-build firms, large subcontractors coordinating trades on BIM-heavy projects, and owners who need visibility across a portfolio of projects also get strong value from ACC's reporting and Insight dashboards. Because pricing and rollout are handled through Autodesk's enterprise sales process, it tends to suit organizations with the internal resources — an IT/BIM manager, a construction technology lead — to manage implementation, permissions, and training across multiple project teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small contractors, solo builders, or residential remodelers looking for a lightweight, self-serve tool for basic scheduling or photo documentation will likely find ACC heavier and more expensive than they need. Its value is closely tied to BIM workflows, so teams that don't design in Autodesk software (or don't work with 3D models at all) lose a significant part of what differentiates ACC from simpler field-management apps.

Organizations that want transparent, published per-seat pricing and an instant online checkout should also be cautious — Autodesk sells ACC through a quote-based, sales-assisted process, and comparing costs against competitors requires a direct conversation rather than a pricing page. Teams without dedicated technical support to handle permissions, module configuration, and change management across a construction organization may find the rollout more demanding than expected.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Autodesk Docs provides the common data environment: version-controlled drawing and document management, markups, and file transmittals that every other ACC module builds on. Autodesk Build layers in day-to-day field management — RFIs, submittals, daily logs, punch lists, quality and safety checklists, and issue tracking — while Autodesk Cost Management (built on the former Assemble Systems technology) connects model-derived quantities to budgets, change orders, and payment applications.

For design and model coordination, Autodesk BIM Collaborate Pro enables multi-discipline clash detection and cloud-based model review, and Autodesk Takeoff supports 2D and 3D quantity takeoff directly from linked models. BuildingConnected extends the platform upstream into bid management and subcontractor prequalification, while Autodesk Insight aggregates project data into portfolio-level dashboards and reporting for executives and owners.

Native mobile apps for iOS and Android let field crews access drawings, log issues, and complete inspections offline, syncing once connectivity returns, and Autodesk Platform Services (formerly Forge) exposes APIs for custom integrations, data extraction, and connecting ACC to other construction or ERP systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Autodesk Construction Cloud Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, ACC feels like a suite of purpose-built modules stitched together rather than one monolithic app — a byproduct of its acquisition-driven history. Users generally find the individual tools (Docs, Build, Takeoff) capable and well-designed on their own, but the overall breadth means new teams face a real learning curve around permissions, module selection, and how data flows between design and field.

Autodesk has invested in unifying account administration, single sign-on, and shared project data across modules, and has been layering in AI-assisted capabilities — such as automated data extraction from drawings and specifications — to reduce manual setup work. Mobile performance in the field is generally regarded as a strength, a legacy of PlanGrid's original focus on reliable offline access to large drawing sets.

Support and training are delivered through Autodesk's enterprise channels, including a certified partner network, documentation, and Autodesk University resources, which suits larger organizations more than teams expecting lightweight, self-service onboarding.

features.title: Common Data Environment (Autodesk Docs)
features.description: Centralized, version-controlled storage for drawings, models, and project documents with markups and transmittal tracking.

features.title: Field Management (Autodesk Build)
features.description: RFIs, submittals, daily logs, punch lists, and quality/safety checklists for on-site construction teams.

features.title: Cost Management
features.description: Model-based quantity takeoff linked to budgets, change orders, and payment applications, built on the former Assemble Systems technology.

features.title: Model Coordination (BIM Collaborate Pro)
features.description: Cloud-based clash detection and multi-discipline model review for design and coordination teams.

features.title: Takeoff
features.description: 2D and 3D quantity takeoff tools that pull directly from linked Autodesk models.

features.title: Bid Management (BuildingConnected)
features.description: Subcontractor prequalification and bid solicitation network for preconstruction teams.

features.title: Insight Dashboards
features.description: Portfolio-level analytics and reporting that aggregate data across projects for executives and owners.

features.title: Mobile Field Apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps with offline access to drawings and forms, syncing when reconnected.

features.title: API / Autodesk Platform Services
features.description: Developer APIs for custom integrations, data extraction, and connections to third-party or ERP systems.

features.title: AI-Assisted Workflows
features.description: AI capabilities that help automate data extraction and analysis from drawings, specifications, and project data.

pros: Deep, native integration with Autodesk design tools like Revit, AutoCAD, and Civil 3D keeps model and field data connected.
Broad module coverage spans design coordination, document control, field management, cost management, and bidding in one ecosystem.
Mobile apps offer reliable offline access to large drawing sets, a strength inherited from PlanGrid.
Portfolio-level reporting via Insight gives owners and executives visibility across multiple projects.
Backed by Autodesk's scale, meaning ongoing investment, a large partner network, and established enterprise support.
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making cost comparisons harder than with self-serve competitors.
The multi-module structure (Docs, Build, Cost Management, Takeoff, BIM Collaborate) can create a learning curve around which module does what.
Value is strongest for teams already using Autodesk design software; non-BIM users may not need its full feature set.
Rollout across a construction organization can require dedicated admin/IT resources to configure permissions and workflows.
As a suite consolidated from several acquisitions, some legacy workflows and interfaces feel less unified than a platform built from a single codebase.

faqs.question: Is Autodesk Construction Cloud the same as BIM 360?
faqs.answer: BIM 360 was Autodesk's earlier cloud collaboration platform. Autodesk Construction Cloud is the successor umbrella that incorporates BIM 360's capabilities alongside PlanGrid, Assemble Systems, and BuildingConnected into a broader connected suite.

faqs.question: How much does Autodesk Construction Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Autodesk does not publish standard per-seat pricing for Construction Cloud. Costs depend on which modules (Docs, Build, Cost Management, Takeoff, BIM Collaborate) and license quantities are selected, and are quoted directly through Autodesk sales or a reseller.

faqs.question: Does Autodesk Construction Cloud integrate with Revit and AutoCAD?
faqs.answer: Yes. As an Autodesk product, ACC is designed to connect directly with Revit, AutoCAD, and Civil 3D, allowing models and drawings to flow into document management, coordination, and field workflows.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial available?
faqs.answer: Autodesk has offered free trials for Construction Cloud products in the past; availability and specific terms should be confirmed on the current construction.autodesk.com site, since trial offerings can change.

faqs.question: What happened to PlanGrid?
faqs.answer: PlanGrid, a field document management app Autodesk acquired in 2018, was integrated into Autodesk Construction Cloud and its capabilities now live primarily within Autodesk Build and Autodesk Docs.

use_cases.title: Field Management for General Contractors
use_cases.description: Track RFIs, submittals, daily logs, and quality/safety inspections from the job site, with mobile offline access for crews.

use_cases.title: Portfolio Oversight for Owners
use_cases.description: Aggregate project data across a capital program into portfolio-level dashboards for reporting and decision-making.

use_cases.title: BIM Coordination for Design-Build Teams
use_cases.description: Run multi-discipline clash detection and model review to keep design and construction teams aligned before issues reach the field.

use_cases.title: Quantity Takeoff and Cost Management for Estimators
use_cases.description: Derive quantities directly from linked 3D models and connect them to budgets, change orders, and payment applications.

features: Common data environment for drawings and documents
